definition: An iterative approach to project management and software development that delivers value incrementally through collaboration and adaptation.
An ordered list of features, bugs, and tasks that represent work to be done on a project. The Product Owner manages the product backlog in Scrum.
The total planned budget for a project. Used as the baseline in Earned Value Management calculations.
The approved plan for a project's scope, schedule, or cost. Used to measure and manage performance deviations.
A formal process for reviewing, approving, and managing changes to project scope, schedule, or cost.
A document that formally authorises a project, identifies the project manager, and defines high-level objectives and constraints.
CPI = EV / AC. A ratio measuring cost efficiency. CPI > 1 means under budget; CPI < 1 means over budget.
The longest sequence of dependent tasks in a project. Any delay on the critical path directly delays the project end date.
Breaking down project deliverables and work into smaller, more manageable components. Used in creating the WBS.
A shared understanding within a Scrum team of what 'complete' means for a product increment. Ensures quality and consistency.
Any unique, verifiable product, result, or capability required to complete a process, phase, or project.
EAC = BAC / CPI. The expected total cost of completing all work. Predicts final project cost based on current performance.
A large user story that is too big to complete in a single sprint. Epics are broken down into smaller user stories.
ETC = EAC - AC. The expected cost to complete all remaining project work from the status date.
The value of work actually performed, expressed in terms of the approved budget assigned to that work.
The amount of time a task can be delayed without delaying the project end date (total float) or the next task (free float).
Adding features or functionality beyond the approved scope without going through change control. Considered a bad practice.
The three primary constraints of a project: Scope, Time, and Cost. Also called the Triple Constraint. Changes to one affect the others.
A time-boxed cycle in Agile during which a team completes a set of work. Synonymous with Sprint in Scrum.
A visual workflow method using cards and columns (To Do, In Progress, Done) to manage work and limit work in progress (WIP).
The first meeting of the project team and stakeholders to officially start the project, align on objectives, and set expectations.
Knowledge gained during a project that helps improve future project performance. Captured throughout and stored in an organisational database.
A significant point or event in a project timeline. Milestones have zero duration and mark the completion of a key deliverable or phase.
A prioritisation technique: Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, Won't Have. Used to prioritise backlog items and requirements.
The simplest version of a product that can be released to gather user feedback. Core concept in Agile and lean development.
An organisational unit that standardises project management processes, provides support, and often governs the project portfolio.
Project Management Body of Knowledge. PMI's guide to PM standards and best practices. The PMP exam is based on its principles.
Project Management Professional. The gold-standard PM certification from PMI. Requires experience, education, and passing a rigorous exam.
In Scrum, the person responsible for maximising the value of the product by managing and prioritising the product backlog.
The authorised budget assigned to scheduled work. Also known as the Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS).
A responsibility assignment chart: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed. Clarifies team member roles on tasks.
A Scrum ceremony at the end of each sprint where the team reflects on their process and identifies improvements.
A document listing identified project risks along with their probability, impact, risk score, and planned response strategies.
The uncontrolled expansion of project scope without adjustments to time, cost, or resources. A leading cause of project failure.
An Agile framework for developing and delivering complex products using time-boxed sprints, defined roles, and regular ceremonies.
The servant-leader of a Scrum team. Removes impediments, facilitates ceremonies, and coaches the team on Scrum practices.
SPI = EV / PV. Measures schedule efficiency. SPI > 1 means ahead of schedule; SPI < 1 means behind schedule.
A time-boxed iteration in Scrum, typically 1–4 weeks, during which the team creates a potentially shippable product increment.
Any individual, group, or organisation that may affect, be affected by, or perceive themselves to be affected by a project.
Testing performed by end users to verify that a system meets their requirements and is ready for deployment.
An informal, natural language description of a feature from an end-user perspective. Format: 'As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit].'
The amount of work a Scrum team can complete in a single sprint, measured in story points. Used to forecast future sprints.
A hierarchical decomposition of the total project scope into deliverables and work packages. The foundation of project planning.
A linear, sequential project management approach where each phase must be completed before the next begins. Best for fixed-scope projects.
Work in Progress limit. In Kanban, a cap on the number of tasks in any given stage to improve flow and reduce multitasking.
